title: The Evolution of Treasury Market Liquidity: Evidence from 30 Years of Limit Order Book Data

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This staff report uses order book and transaction data from the U.S. Treasury securities market to construct a daily liquidity index over a 30-year sample period (1991–2021). The index incorporates bid-ask spreads, quoted depth, and price impact to capture different dimensions of market liquidity. The authors find that in March 2020, the liquidity index peaked at levels commensurate with the 2007–09 global financial crisis, while other liquidity proxies showed much lower peaks. Key drivers of market liquidity include announcements, implied volatility, and the presence of high-frequency traders.
key_points: Authors: Tobias Adrian, Michael J. Fleming, and Erik Vogt; Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report No. 827, revised January 2023
The liquidity index is constructed from bid-ask spreads, quoted depth, and price impact, and is more sensitive to short-term liquidity drivers than prior proxies
The March 2020 liquidity stress is documented as peak level equivalent to the 2007–09 global financial crisis
Other liquidity proxies peak at much lower levels than the index during March 2020, suggesting they understate contemporaneous stress
Significant drivers of Treasury market liquidity: announcements, implied volatility, and high-frequency trader presence
The report is available only in PDF format
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical foundations for understanding Treasury market liquidity dynamics over three decades, including during systemic stress events. It quantifies how high-frequency trading affects liquidity and demonstrates that commonly used liquidity measures may understate true stress conditions. For policymakers and market participants, this offers calibration tools for assessing real-time market health.
market_structure_context: The paper is directly focused on market structure, examining Treasury market liquidity measurement using limit order book data. It studies how bid-ask spreads, quoted depth, and price impact evolve over 30 years and how these are affected by high-frequency traders, volatility regimes, and announcements.

human_review.notes: Source is a historical research paper (revised 2023) but highly relevant to market structure.
